reasoning:
The 7-year Treasury yield dropped sharply to 3.97% on Friday, August 1, 2025, driven by a weaker-than-expected July jobs report and political turmoil (the firing of the BLS Commissioner and a Fed governor resignation). This massive one-day decline (down roughly 17 bps) positions the yield below recent trends. The FRED statistical baseline gives a 54% probability of exceeding this level by August 10, capturing a standard mean-reversion dynamic following large single-day shocks. I rely on this 54% as my primary anchor, estimating a 56% chance of an increase by August 10 due to potential stabilization and the possibility that newly announced tariffs could inject an inflation premium, steepening the curve. 

For longer horizons, uncertainty significantly increases. The weaker labor market strongly implies the Federal Reserve will embark on deeper rate cuts, putting structural downward pressure on yields. This competes against the potential for higher term premiums brought by political instability and tariff-driven inflation risks. Aligning with the requirement that uncertainty must increase over time, my probabilities revert smoothly toward 0.50 for the distant dates.
